item.title: CVE-2026-42824 SearchLeak, ShinyHunters Breaches, Check Point VPN Exploitation, and AUR Supply-Chain Malware
item.description: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software...
item.content:encoded:
Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software ecosystems, browser extensions, SaaS platforms, and identity workflows to enable credential theft, data exposure, or stealthy persistence. The most significant technical disclosures centered on Microsoft 365 Copilot SearchLeak, Check Point Remote Access VPN exploitation, LangGraph remote code execution research, and a Linux package supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository. In parallel, ShinyHunters-linked breaches and Baltic Sea subsea cable damage proceedings highlighted ongoing risks to institutional data and critical infrastructure. Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a critical vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search tracked as CVE-2026-42824. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions and silently exfiltrate indexed organizational data after a victim clicked a crafted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link. In the demonstrated SearchLeak flow, Copilot could be instructed to search a victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed content, then place extracted data into an image URL. The browser request went to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, which fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen information; Microsoft patched the issue and assigned it a maximum critical severity rating. Check Point Research also reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The same reporting noted exploitable LangGraph flaws, where SQL injection and unsafe deserialization in affected checkpointer components could be chained to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued. ShinyHunters activity featured prominently across multiple reports. The University of Nottingham disclosed that the group accessed its student records system, affecting approximately 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records. Infinite Campus also reported a Salesforce-targeted data theft incident attributed to ShinyHunters, affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Have I Been Pwned said the leaked data included names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets, while Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised. These incidents reflect a broader pattern of attackers targeting SaaS-hosted business records and support systems rather than only core production databases. The data described in both cases included personally identifiable information and institutional records useful for downstream phishing, impersonation, and social engineering. Researchers reported a compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, where administrative privileges were available, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems. The campaign highlights the risk of trusted community-maintained package ecosystems being abused for both userland credential theft and deeper kernel-level persistence. A separate browser ecosystem investigation identified 152 Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons with a combined 105,000 installs. Socket linked the extensions to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, noting discrepancies between Chrome Web Store claims that user data was not collected and privacy policies describing logging of IP addresses, ISPs, click counts, and referrers. Phishing and credential capture also appeared in targeted email reporting from IRONSCALES, which described a PDF-based lure sent to a security company CEO with a fabricated email thread and a “Review and Sign” button. The message passed DKIM but failed DMARC strict alignment because the signing domain did not align with the header From domain; Microsoft assigned SCL=5 and quarantined it. Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg over alleged damage to two subsea telecommunications cables and attempted damage to eight other Baltic Sea connections. Authorities said the vessel dragged a damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers, while the defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction. OT risk reporting focused on the difficulty of prioritizing vulnerabilities in industrial environments where devices may remain operational despite serious exposure. The coverage emphasized that long CVE lists and technical alert rankings often fail to convey which weaknesses could affect production, safety, or quality in a specific plant environment. AI governance also surfaced as Anthropic said it disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. government export control directive barred foreign nationals from accessing them. Anthropic said the government appeared concerned about a jailbreak method but provided only verbal evidence, while the company characterized the identified issues as minor, previously known, and reproducible in other public models.

SearchLeak: How We Turned M365 Copilot Into a One-Click Data Exfiltration Weapon — Should Read

Varonis researchers disclosed SearchLeak, a now-patched critical Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise vulnerability chain that could let attackers exfiltrate a user’s emails, files, calendar data, and other organizational content with a single click on a trusted Microsoft link. The attack combined prompt injection, unsafe streaming HTML rendering, and a Bing SSRF/CSP bypass to silently send sensitive data to an attacker-controlled server.

  • Varonis Threat Labs uncovered SearchLeak, a three-stage vulnerability chain that turned Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search into a silent data exfiltration mechanism.
  • SearchLeak combined Parameter-to-Prompt Injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery used to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions.
  • The attack could be triggered when a victim clicked a trusted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link, causing Copilot to search the victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed organizational content.
  • During the attack, Copilot generated an image tag containing extracted data, the browser sent the request to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, and Bing fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen data.
  • Microsoft patched the vulnerability under CVE-2026-42824 and assigned it a maximum severity rating of critical.

Infinite Campus data breach affects 137,000 school staff accounts — Should Read

ShinyHunters stole personal and contact information from 137,100 school staff accounts by targeting Infinite Campus’s Salesforce instance. Infinite Campus says the exposed data was mostly directory-style staff information and that customer databases were not compromised.

  • The ShinyHunters extortion gang stole personal information from more than 137,000 school staff accounts in a March Salesforce data theft attack targeting Infinite Campus.
  • Infinite Campus said the attacker targeted its Salesforce instance and that the exposed data consisted largely of names and contact information for school staff, mostly directory information commonly found on school websites.
  • Have I Been Pwned reported that the leaked data exposed 137,100 accounts, including unique names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets.
  • ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for the breach on its data leak site and leaked a 1.2GB archive allegedly containing Salesforce records with personally identifiable information and other internal corporate data.
  • Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised.

Anthropic says US government forced it to disable cybersecurity AI models — Should Read

Anthropic says a U.S. government export-control directive forced it to disable its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity AI models for all customers, including foreign nationals in the U.S. The company disputes the government’s stated jailbreak concerns and says the action was not transparent or technically justified.

  • Anthropic disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused AI models after receiving a U.S. government export control directive barring foreign nationals from accessing them.
  • Anthropic said the directive applied to foreign nationals both inside the United States and abroad, including the company’s own employees.
  • Anthropic said the government appears to believe it has become aware of a method for jailbreaking Fable 5, but the company said it received only verbal evidence of the technique.
  • Anthropic said its review found the identified vulnerabilities were minor, previously known, and reproducible using other publicly available models including OpenAI's GPT-5.5.
  • Anthropic said it supports government authority to block unsafe AI deployments only through a transparent, fair, clear statutory process grounded in technical facts.

152 Chrome Wallpaper Extensions with 105K Installs Linked to Adware and Fake Traffic — Should Read

Researchers found 152 Chrome live-wallpaper extensions with about 105,000 installs that behaved like adware by tracking users despite store claims and generating fake “organic” Google traffic. The campaign appears financially motivated, using install and uninstall redirects to manipulate traffic attribution and share data with ad partners.

  • Cybersecurity researchers discovered 152 Google Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons that distribute a potentially unwanted program family.
  • The extensions span 38 Chrome Web Store publisher accounts, use the tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, and chromewallpaper.com backends, and have been installed 105,000 times collectively.
  • Socket said the Chrome Web Store listings claimed the extensions did not collect or use user data, while linked privacy policies stated that they log IP addresses, ISP, click counts, and referrers and share data with ad partners.
  • Some extensions used hard-coded install and uninstall URLs to make extension-driven visits appear as Google organic search traffic or genuine Google Search activity.
  • Socket assessed the campaign as a financially motivated commercial adware and traffic-attribution-fraud affiliate operation, with circumstantial indicators suggesting a possible origin in Turkey.

Finland brings charges against cargo ship officers for cutting submarine cables — May Read

Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo ship Fitburg with damaging two Baltic Sea subsea telecom cables and attempting to damage eight others by allegedly dragging an anchor for at least 130 kilometers. The defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction because the cable damage occurred outside Finnish territorial waters.

  • The Finnish Prosecution Service announced charges against the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg for allegedly damaging two subsea telecommunications cables and attempting to damage eight other subsea connections in the Baltic Sea.
  • Finnish authorities seized the Fitburg after cable faults were detected, and Finnish Customs said the ship had been transporting sanctioned steel products from Russia to Israel.
  • Prosecutors said the Fitburg is believed to have dragged its damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers until Finnish authorities halted the vessel’s movement.
  • Both defendants deny committing the offenses and are expected to argue that Finland lacks jurisdiction because the cable damage occurred outside Finnish territorial waters.
  • The case follows an earlier failed attempt to prosecute senior officers of the Russia-linked Eagle S tanker, whose alleged cable damage case collapsed amid legal rulings disputing Finland’s jurisdiction.

The Distribution Agreement That Arrived with a Thread Full of Latin — May Read

A phishing email from a compromised legitimate domain used a PDF “distribution agreement” to hide a credential-harvesting lure inside a fake internal email thread. Strict DMARC alignment failed the otherwise valid DKIM signature, and Microsoft/IRONSCALES quarantined it as a high-risk spearphishing attempt.

  • A phishing email from accounting2@gasparinutrition[.]com targeted the CEO of a security company with the subject “Completed: Signature is required on Distribution Agreement.”
  • The email body contained only a confidentiality disclaimer, while the payload was a PDF attachment titled as a distribution agreement with a “Review and Sign” button.
  • The PDF fabricated a four-reply email thread using the target organization’s real domain and employee-style names, but the thread text contained Latin placeholder content generated by a phishing kit.
  • The message passed DKIM verification, but DMARC failed because the DKIM signing domain under gappssmtp[.]com did not strictly align with the header From domain gasparinutrition[.]com.
  • Microsoft scored the message at SCL=5 and quarantined it, while Themis identified the credential theft pattern and VIP targeting.

The Onboarding Password Mistake That Creates Unnecessary Risk — Optional

The article warns that sharing temporary onboarding passwords by email, SMS, or phone creates avoidable risk because those credentials can be intercepted, mishandled, or left unchanged. It promotes letting new employees securely create their own first-day passwords to reduce exposure and simplify onboarding.

  • Organizations often send temporary first-day passwords to new employees by email, SMS, or phone, which can expose credentials if messages are intercepted, forwarded, accessed on unsecured devices, or relayed by third parties.
  • The article says temporary onboarding passwords can become long-term weaknesses when users do not change them, workflows fail to enforce resets, or the credentials remain active unnoticed.
  • Specops First Day Password, available as part of Specops uReset, lets new employees set their own password through a secure enrollment process instead of receiving a temporary credential.
  • In November 2023, Cyber Av3ngers exploited Unitronics PLCs using the default credential “1111” at the Municipal Water Authority of Aliquippa in Pennsylvania.
  • In 2025, researchers found that McDonald’s McHire platform could be accessed through a weak legacy administrator account reportedly using “123456” as both the username and password.

The Invisible Problem: Why OT Cyber Risk Is Hard to Prioritise — Optional

OT cyber risk is hard to prioritise because vulnerabilities often remain invisible while operations continue normally, making them less urgent than visible production issues. The key is translating technical findings into clear operational impact so teams know what matters and what action is sensible.

  • OT cyber risk is difficult to prioritise because vulnerable devices can continue operating normally without visible signs of failure.
  • Operational leaders must balance cybersecurity remediation against production schedules and the risk that poorly executed fixes may create larger problems.
  • Long lists of CVEs and technically ranked alerts often add noise rather than helping plant managers decide what to do.
  • OT teams need clearer information about which vulnerabilities could affect production, safety, or quality in their specific environment.
  • The article argues that passive monitoring and continuous assessment can help translate technical exposure into operational impact.

15th June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

This week’s threat report highlights active exploitation of major enterprise vulnerabilities, including Oracle PeopleSoft attacks tied to ShinyHunters and a Check Point VPN authentication bypass linked to Qilin ransomware. It also flags rising risks around AI tools, supply-chain compromises, and urgent patches from Microsoft and Veeam.

  • The University of Nottingham suffered a data breach after ShinyHunters accessed its student records system, affecting about 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records.
  • Check Point Research identified active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol.
  • Microsoft released its largest Patch Tuesday update to date, addressing more than 200 Windows and Defender vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-45657, CVE-2026-41091, and CVE-2026-50507.
  • Check Point Research demonstrated exploitable flaws in LangGraph by chaining SQL injection and unsafe deserialization issues to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued for affected checkpointer components.
  • Researchers detected a supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository where attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, with administrative privileges, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems.

Webinar: How behavioral AI stops phishing and account takeovers — Optional

BleepingComputer will host a July 8, 2026 webinar with Abnormal AI and Novant Health on using behavioral AI to detect and automate response to phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks that bypass traditional email security and MFA controls.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will be presented by Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • The session will examine how phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email defenses.
  • The article says attackers increasingly abuse legitimate authentication flows and compromised accounts to evade detection and maintain persistent access.
  • The webinar will cover how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows for inbound email threats and compromised accounts.

item.title: CVE-2026-0257 Exploitation, Apache HTTP/2 DoS, Nuxt and UniFi Flaws
item.description: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 in PAN-OS GlobalProtect, alongside multiple pre-authentication flaws that could affect exposed web and management surfaces. Palo Alto Networks reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS GlobalProtect. The company said an unknown threat actor used the flaw to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals, with initial observed activity dating to May 17, 2026, and limited exploitation resulting in some VPN sessions being established. The vulnerability was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, reflecting confirmed exploitation in the wild. Palo Alto Networks stated that it had not identified post-access behavior or lateral movement in the observed cases, and that only a small portion of probed devices successfully established VPN connections. Apache HTTP Server was also highlighted for CVE-2026-49975, a denial-of-service vulnerability in the modhttp2 module affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67 when HTTP/2 is enabled. The reported attack combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold, causing rapid memory growth while keeping allocations alive; Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 was released on June 8, 2026, with a fix for this and other vulnerabilities. Nuxt applications are affected by CVE-2026-53721, a route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher. The issue can be triggered before authentication and without user interaction by altering URL path casing, such as requesting /Admin instead of /admin, and affects Nuxt 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7. Ubiquiti UniFi OS was reported vulnerable to CVE-2026-47368, a path traversal issue caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input during file path construction. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can allow attackers with network access to read arbitrary files, including configuration files, logs, and sensitive application data. Affected UniFi products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, Express line versions before 4.0.15, and UDM family versions before 5.1.15. Ubiquiti published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, making this another example of exposure risk concentrated around management interfaces and edge-adjacent infrastructure. Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers. The campaigns were attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month. Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure. The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, with Group-IB observing the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams across multiple regions. The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to send victims toward premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams. Separately, Constella described how OSINT teams use identity intelligence to correlate emails, usernames, phone numbers, credentials, social accounts, breach data, infrastructure, and public records to accelerate attribution, exposure analysis, relationship mapping, and investigative triage.

item.title: FBI Disrupts Outsider Enterprise AI-Powered Phishing-as-a-Service Operation
item.description: This digest window is dominated by a major law enforcement disruption of a large-scale phishing-as-a-service ecosystem attributed to a Chinese operation known as Outsider Enterprise. Reporting indicates the service industrialized credential and payment-card theft through thousands of phishing...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by a major law enforcement disruption of a large-scale phishing-as-a-service ecosystem attributed to a Chinese operation known as Outsider Enterprise. Reporting indicates the service industrialized credential and payment-card theft through thousands of phishing sites, more than a million fraudulent URLs, SMS delivery at scale, and AI-enabled tooling. The case reflects continued convergence between cybercrime infrastructure, mobile messaging abuse, automation, and hosted e-commerce-style service models. It also highlights growing collaboration between law enforcement, cloud and internet infrastructure providers, telecommunications carriers, and security researchers in disrupting fraud operations before messages and phishing links reach end users. The FBI, working with Google and Black Lotus Labs, dismantled infrastructure tied to Outsider Enterprise, a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation accused of running thousands of fraudulent websites designed to steal credit card data and passwords. Google linked the operation to approximately 9,000 fake websites and more than one million fraudulent URLs, illustrating the scale at which the group generated and distributed phishing infrastructure. Authorities believe campaigns connected to Outsider Enterprise stole more than 3.8 million credit card records and caused an estimated $1.9 billion in losses. During the disruption, the FBI and partners seized multiple administration servers, a Shopify storefront, a testing account, roughly $100,000 in USDT, and a Telegram bot associated with the operation. The seized assets point to a mature service model spanning web hosting, testing workflows, payment infrastructure, and operator communications. Google also filed a civil lawsuit targeting the operation’s infrastructure and said it is coordinating with AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon to block fraudulent messages before they reach subscribers. According to Google, Outsider Enterprise sent 2.5 million SMS messages to Android users over a two-week period in May, and Android users flagged 55,000 of those messages as fraudulent. The reported SMS volume underscores the role of mobile delivery channels in modern phishing operations and the operational emphasis on rapid, high-volume lure distribution. The reporting characterizes Outsider Enterprise as AI-powered, reflecting the broader trend of fraud services adopting automation and generative tooling to scale phishing content, infrastructure, and campaign management. While the article focuses on the takedown rather than technical indicators, the scope of domains, URLs, SMS traffic, and stolen payment records makes the operation one of the more significant recent examples of phishing-as-a-service disruption.

item.title: InformData-Impersonation Phishing Passed DMARC via Authorized SendGrid Infrastructure
item.description: This digest covers a phishing case in which email authentication controls validated the sending path but did not establish message legitimacy. The reported activity highlights a common detection challenge for security teams: attacker abuse or misconfiguration of authorized third-party email...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a phishing case in which email authentication controls validated the sending path but did not establish message legitimacy. The reported activity highlights a common detection challenge for security teams: attacker abuse or misconfiguration of authorized third-party email infrastructure can allow malicious messages to pass SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks even under strict enforcement. IRONSCALES reported an employment verification-themed phishing email impersonating InformData that was sent through SendGrid infrastructure and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, including a DMARC policy set to REJECT. The message was associated with SendGrid host wfbtrqkw.outbound-mail.sendgrid.net and IP address 159[.]183[.]84[.]25, which the article states were configured as authorized sending infrastructure for international.informdata[.]com. The phishing lure targeted a sports data technology company and was flagged in June 2026 after a colleague at the organization reported the email. According to IRONSCALES, one mailbox was quarantined. The message used an employment verification pretext while redirecting replies and user interaction away from the apparent sender’s legitimate domain. The email’s Reply-To header directed responses to support@verifying[.]you, while the call-to-action linked to verify.zippedscript[.]com; both were described by IRONSCALES as attacker-controlled domains. This combination created a mismatch between the authenticated sending domain and the destinations used for follow-up interaction, allowing the message to appear technically authenticated while steering the recipient into attacker-controlled communication paths. The case illustrates the distinction between domain authentication and message intent. As described in the report, DMARC validated that the message was sent via infrastructure authorized for the domain, but it did not determine whether the email content, Reply-To destination, or embedded link represented a legitimate business process. For defenders and threat hunters, the incident is notable because the malicious indicators were not authentication failures, but rather the use of authorized infrastructure alongside suspicious reply routing and external verification domains.

item.title: Outsider Enterprise Takedown and DMARC-Passing Employment Verification Phishing
item.description: This 24-hour digest window is dominated by phishing infrastructure and delivery abuse, spanning both large-scale phishing-as-a-service operations and targeted business-themed credential or data-harvesting lures. The reporting highlights how attackers are combining trusted platforms, authenticated...
item.content:encoded: This 24-hour digest window is dominated by phishing infrastructure and delivery abuse, spanning both large-scale phishing-as-a-service operations and targeted business-themed credential or data-harvesting lures. The reporting highlights how attackers are combining trusted platforms, authenticated sending paths, per-recipient tracking, and high-volume messaging to improve delivery and attribution of victim engagement. The FBI, working with Google and Black Lotus Labs, disrupted a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation tracked as Outsider Enterprise. Google linked the operation to approximately 9,000 fake websites and more than one million fraudulent URLs used to steal credit card data and passwords. Authorities believe campaigns associated with the service stole more than 3.8 million credit card records and caused an estimated $1.9 billion in losses. The takedown reportedly included the seizure of multiple administration servers, a Shopify storefront, a testing account, roughly $100,000 in USDT, and a Telegram bot connected to the operation. Google also filed a civil lawsuit targeting the group’s infrastructure and said it is coordinating with AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon to block fraudulent messages before they reach subscribers. Google reported that Outsider Enterprise sent 2.5 million SMS messages to Android users during a two-week period in May, with Android users flagging 55,000 of them as fraudulent. In a separate phishing case, attackers impersonated InformData in an employment verification-themed email sent from verifications@international.informdata[.]com using SendGrid infrastructure. The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC despite the domain having a REJECT enforcement policy, because the sending path was authorized for international.informdata[.]com. The campaign illustrates that email authentication success does not necessarily indicate benign intent when legitimate or authorized infrastructure is abused. The employment verification lure observed by IRONSCALES used a Reply-To address at support@verifying[.]you, while its call-to-action directed recipients to verify.zippedscript[.]com. Both domains were described as attacker-controlled, creating a diversion between the authenticated sender identity and the infrastructure used for follow-up interaction. The campaign’s structure allowed the email to appear technically aligned while shifting recipient replies and clicks away from the impersonated brand domain. The call-to-action URL included a long per-recipient tracking token that could allow the attacker to correlate clicks with specific email addresses. IRONSCALES classified the message as high risk based on behavioral indicators including a Reply-To domain mismatch, a first-time sender pattern, and mismatched link destinations. The case underscores the continued use of human resources and employment verification workflows as credible business pretexts for phishing. Together, these reports show phishing operations operating at both industrial and targeted scales. Outsider Enterprise demonstrates the breadth of criminal phishing ecosystems built around mass URL generation, SMS delivery, and payment-card theft, while the InformData impersonation case shows how adversaries can exploit trusted email services and authentication alignment to increase the apparent legitimacy of business-themed lures.

item.title: Former Saydel School IT Employee Sentenced for Post-Employment Hacks
item.description: This digest window centers on an insider-threat case involving a former school district IT employee who retained access after leaving the organization and used it to disrupt systems over an extended period. The reporting highlights the operational impact of lingering administrative access in...
item.content:encoded: This digest window centers on an insider-threat case involving a former school district IT employee who retained access after leaving the organization and used it to disrupt systems over an extended period. The reporting highlights the operational impact of lingering administrative access in education environments, including disruption to identity, device management, learning platforms, and public-facing accounts. A former senior IT support specialist for Saydel Community School District, Ezekiel Dean Potter, was sentenced to 21 months in prison for cyberattacks against his former employer. Prosecutors said Potter retained credentials after his employment ended and targeted district systems over a 21-month period, causing disruptions across administrative, instructional, and communications platforms. According to court documents, Potter deleted the district’s Facebook page and disrupted access to educational platforms and accounts. He also deleted data from the district’s Apple School Manager account, which prevented staff from accessing the platform and disabled management of district MacBooks and iPads for roughly a week. The case also involved compromise of Google administrator accounts used to access the district’s Schoology learning management system. In January 2025, Potter used that access to delete employee Gmail accounts, disrupting teacher access and broader district operations. Potter was ordered to serve three years of supervised release following his prison term and to pay $59,668.81 in restitution to the Saydel Community School District and its insurer. The restitution reflects remediation costs associated with restoring affected services and addressing the damage caused by the unauthorized activity.

item.title: Velvet Ant Operation Highland Backdoored Critical Infrastructure Authentication for a Decade
item.description: This digest centers on a long-running Chinese-linked espionage operation targeting an isolated critical infrastructure network. Reporting from Sygnia describes how Velvet Ant sustained access for approximately 10 years by compromising authentication paths, modifying trusted Linux components, and...
item.content:encoded: This digest centers on a long-running Chinese-linked espionage operation targeting an isolated critical infrastructure network. Reporting from Sygnia describes how Velvet Ant sustained access for approximately 10 years by compromising authentication paths, modifying trusted Linux components, and creating covert remote execution routes into a segregated environment. The case highlights a mature persistence model focused less on noisy malware deployment and more on deep integration with identity, access, and network control planes. For technical defenders and executives alike, the incident illustrates how isolation alone can be undermined when authentication flows and administrative infrastructure are subverted. Sygnia attributed the campaign, dubbed Operation Highland, to the Chinese-linked Velvet Ant threat group. The activity reportedly affected a large organization’s isolated critical infrastructure network, where the attackers maintained espionage access for a decade. The operation began with compromise of internet-facing systems before the attackers pivoted inward using a modified GS-Netcat reverse shell and a custom SOCKS5 proxy. The campaign’s most significant feature was the abuse of authentication and application infrastructure to reach systems that were not directly exposed. Velvet Ant modified Nginx and FastCGI configurations to create a remote-execution pathway into the segregated environment through crafted HTTP requests. This allowed interaction with the isolated network without requiring a direct connection to the critical infrastructure segment. Sygnia also reported that the attackers replaced legitimate Linux PAM modules and OpenSSH components with trojanized versions. These backdoored components accepted hardcoded passwords, harvested credentials, captured commands, and preserved persistent access. By embedding themselves into core authentication mechanisms, the operators gained durable control over administrative pathways while blending into expected system behavior. The operation demonstrates a persistence strategy built around the compromise of trusted binaries and configuration layers rather than reliance on standalone implants alone. Backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components gave Velvet Ant access at the point where users and administrators authenticated, creating opportunities to collect credentials and observe operator activity. The use of modified web and FastCGI configurations further expanded the attackers’ ability to execute commands through infrastructure that would normally be considered part of legitimate service delivery. Remediation was described as complex because the compromised components were central to system authentication and remote administration. Sygnia noted that replacing many trojanized critical components could risk breaking authentication, locking out legitimate administrators, or causing operational outages. In the context of critical infrastructure, that operational fragility made eradication more difficult than simply removing a conventional malware payload. Overall, Operation Highland reflects an espionage pattern in which adversaries target the connective tissue between exposed enterprise systems and protected operational environments. The reported decade-long access underscores the strategic value of authentication infrastructure, reverse proxies, and administrative services as targets for stealthy persistence inside high-value networks.
